data.title: RING: Growing Gold Demand May Help Lift This ETF
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier this month, I wrote about the abrdn Physical Precious Metals Basket Shares ETF (GLTR) and in the article I discussed the price of gold as well as the state of the gold market. At the time, precious metals markets were caught in a bout of hysteria amidst trade and tariff uncertainty. But what a difference a couple of weeks can make.

To be sure, liquidity is still flowing into the sector, but much of the frenzy that was on display, especially by physical metals traders, has abated. This has a lot to do with the Trump administration's decision to exempt precious metals from reciprocal import tariffs.

That news alone was enough to crush the premium at which Comex gold future's trade over the corresponding contract on the London Metals Exchange, taking it down to $20/oz from $43/oz the day before. Comex gold inventories, which had reached record levels, have also since begun to thin as some of the yellow metal is shipped back to Europe.

But while the froth that was clearly evident In the market during this most recent episode may have diminished, the long-term case for gold remains intact. And the underlying drivers for this bull market are still very much operative, making the iShares MSCI Global Gold Miners ETF (NASDAQ:RING) a good vehicle for investors wishing to gain exposure to the sector.

The Gold Market

Gold demand remains healthy as central banks resumed their buying this year after taking a bit of a break in December. February saw another positive month of buying, with net purchases of 24t.

The central bankers have been a vital pillar of gold demand for a decade and a half now, being net purchasers on a yearly basis since 2010. But they are increasingly being joined by retail investors who over the course of the past year have gone from being net sellers to being net buyers, and their pace has been accelerating. As can be seen in the exhibit below, ETF inflows were almost 52 tonnes last week, or about $4.7 billion, and that trend has clearly been on the rise over recent months.

And a close inspection of the chart above also reveals that a growing proportion of those inflows are coming from Asian investors, especially China, where demand is growing quickly. In fact, the Chinese government, which tightly controls the amount of gold entering and leaving the country, recently issued additional import quotas to some of its commercial banks in response to strong institutional and retail demand. The government is also testing out a program to let insurance funds invest in bullion in order to more broadly diversify their portfolio holdings.

So, as the gold buying frenzy in New York and London may have cooled in the past weeks, it's still alive and well in Shanghai; in fact, gold buying-fervor may have actually grown in China during the last few weeks, especially after the most recent round of tariffs and counter-tariffs between Washington and Beijing. While the Comex/LME premium was narrowing, the Shanghai premium over gold in international markets flipped to positive after being negative for most of last year.

And given the rising geopolitical tensions and growing animosity between Beijing and Washington, it is probable that Chinese gold demand will remain elevated in the months to come.

RING

The iShares MSCI Global Gold Miners ETF is a solid fund for any investor wishing to gain exposure to the gold sector. The 13-year-old fund, which pays a semiannual distribution and currently yields about 1%, has assets of just over $1.4 billion and charges a very reasonable 0.39% Total Expense ratio.

It tracks the MSCI ACWI Select Gold Miners Investable Market Index and currently has 39 holdings. Its 10 largest positions, which constitute about 72% of the fund, are a who's who of the world's major gold mining companies. Its two largest positions are premiere North American miners, which have seen some solid returns as of late. Granted, Newmont Corp. (NEM) has lagged on a full-year basis after its stock pulled back in October, but Agnico Eagle Mines' (AEM) stock performance over the last year has been excellent.

When it comes to geographic distribution, RING has more than half of its holdings in Canadian-domiciled firms, but it also has exposure to many of the world's other top mining jurisdictions. However, given the size of some of these companies and the global distribution of their projects, geographic distribution may be somewhat less important than it would be for a portfolio of pure play single-asset miners.

Risk

The main sources of risk for this thesis are twofold. Firstly, any major and prolonged pullback in the price of bullion will probably lead to considerable deterioration in the fund's NAV. Also, the ETF is subject to some operational risk from its principal holdings. Given the size of its Newmont and Agnico positions, any sharp fall back in the price of either stock stemming from operational or other corporate issues would likely lead to a negative impact on the fund's unit price.

Takeaway

While gold trading may have calmed somewhat in New York and London, the pace of buying and selling has increased considerably in Shanghai. And all of this goes to show that gold is still the go-to asset for central bankers, institutional investors, and retail traders whenever they sense a disturbance in the macroeconomic or geopolitical force. For those reasons, I remain bullish on the metal as well as on gold miners; for those reason, I rate the RING ETF as a Buy.

data.title: How U.S. Tariffs Could Impact Latin America
data.description:
Most Latin American countries will be subject to the 10% baseline tariff imposed by US President Donald Trump that entered into force on April 9, with higher levies for Guyana (38%), Nicaragua (18%) and Venezuela (15%), although these have been paused for 90 days. Mexico is subject to a different tariff structure, with a 25% tariff implemented in March, although with significant exceptions.

Our baseline scenario has assumed since December 2024 a 10% universal tariff on all countries/territories in the region, except Mexico, for which we projected a 25% tariff in our March round. As this has proved accurate, we will not significantly alter our forecasts for most markets, based on the direct impact of the new US tariffs.

We also anticipate second-round effects, as growth in trading partners outside the region, such as the US, mainland China and Europe, is also likely to be significantly reduced by the tariffs. Based on preliminary estimates, the forecast for global growth in 2025 will be revised downward by four-tenths of a percentage point due to the tariffs announced on April 2. This adjustment is primarily driven by anticipated declines in growth in the US and in mainland China.

A more pronounced global slowdown than previously anticipated would adversely affect our real GDP growth forecasts for the region, as multiple transmission channels — trade, foreign direct investment and remittances — would be impacted.

Uncertainty surrounding tariffs and potential bilateral negotiations between the US and its trade partners is likely to delay investment decisions. Latin American countries are mainly commodity exporters, and a slowdown in global trade and global economic growth is likely to soften commodity prices, reducing export and fiscal revenues for exporters of oil (such as Brazil, Mexico and Colombia), soybeans (Brazil and Argentina), and copper (Chile and Peru).

The US widely ranks as the largest or second-largest trading partner for several countries in the region. The region’s reliance on US trade and the fact that no country has announced countermeasures to date support our view that reciprocal tariffs are unlikely. Instead, Latin American countries are likely to seek negotiations to reduce or postpone the implementation of tariffs or to exempt specific products.

Most countries have limited economic leverage to reciprocate and are more likely to offer concessions on security, such as combating criminal organizations and drug trafficking; strengthening illegal migration control; and increasing support with deportations of their migrants from the US, particularly Mexico, Central America and northern South American countries.

In South America, resource-rich countries, such as Argentina, Chile and Peru, are likely to offer increased investment opportunities in key sectors, such as mining, energy and infrastructure, or eliminate existing trade barriers for certain US products.

Higher impact: Mexico and Venezuela

A previously announced 25% universal tariff affecting Mexico entered into force on March 12, although the country was exempted from the new 10% tariff. Mexico had successfully negotiated partial waivers, with exports under the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA) exempt, while automotive exports incur the 25% tariff only on foreign content from outside North America.

This led us to revise our projection of a 25% universal tariff for Mexico down to an average of approximately 10.5%. This adjustment is likely to permit us to raise our forecast for Mexican real GDP growth in 2025. The amended tariff configuration will also help Mexico maintain a competitive edge as a source country for the US market, especially vis-à-vis Asian markets. Mexican officials are negotiating exemptions to the 25% tariffs on steel, aluminum and automotive imports, with progress likely to lower the probability of countermeasures.

For Venezuela, the US had previously announced secondary tariffs of 25% on all countries importing Venezuelan oil. If these tariffs are implemented at end-May, as scheduled, and major buyers like mainland China, India, Spain and Italy reduce crude imports, Venezuela would face a major reduction in foreign exchange revenues.

We forecast that the combined impact of US oil sanctions and new tariffs will decrease Venezuela’s GDP in 2025, compared with the current baseline forecast of an expansion. This revenue loss and economic decline would be unsustainable and potentially destabilizing for the Maduro administration.

In response, Venezuela would likely seek to increase black-market oil sales to mainland China at discounted prices, although these are unlikely to fully offset revenue losses. The government also probably would seek to comply more fully with US demands for the repatriation of Venezuelan migrants in the US to facilitate negotiations to permit the continuation of US-bound oil exports.

Medium impact: Argentina and Brazil

As members of the Mercosur trading bloc, Argentina and Brazil have higher overall tariffs on US imports than the other way around. Although Brazil’s Congress has approved a bill allowing the government to impose reciprocal tariffs, President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva is likely to prioritize negotiations with the US before considering countermeasures. Brazilian exports to the US make up about 11% of the total exports; therefore, the new 10% tariffs are unlikely to significantly hinder Brazilian economic growth.

Argentine President Javier Milei closely aligns with Trump and will avoid antagonizing the US. His administration is currently negotiating a trade deal with the US to reduce tariffs on approximately 50 Argentine products, including agricultural and fishing produce. In 2024, Argentine exports to the US accounted for 8% of its total, with the hydrocarbon sector making up a quarter and remaining exempt from US tariffs.

We do not foresee significant changes in forecast Argentine GDP growth, as the 10% general tariff was already factored into our forecast for 2025. Officials from both Argentina and Brazil are currently negotiating the previously imposed 25% tariffs on steel and aluminum, seeking exemptions, reductions or, in the case of Brazil, a duty-free quota.

Lower impact: Chile, Colombia and Peru

Tariffs are likely to have a limited impact on Chile, Colombia and Peru, the only South American countries with free trade agreements (FTAs) with the US. Their primary exports to the US — copper for Chile and Peru, and oil for Colombia — remain exempt from tariffs as the Trump administration continues its review, and we anticipate minimal economic impact.

We have maintained GDP growth forecasts for Colombia, Chile and for Peru in 2025. These three countries are likely to negotiate with the US through institutional channels and diplomatic engagement, with Chilean officials announcing plans to request consultations under the FTA. They will probably offer increased investment opportunities to US firms

Expanding to new markets or deepening trade links with existing trade partners is also probable. Chile and Peru are unlikely to reduce their trade with mainland China, their primary trading partner and buyer of copper.

In early April, Chilean President Gabriel Boric signed agreements with India to enhance cooperation on this mineral, including long-term supply for Indian companies, strengthening supply chains and promoting investment. These countries are also likely to explore opportunities to leverage their lower US tariffs versus competitors’ in other sectors if the higher tariffs are effectively activated after the 90-day pause, in areas like Colombian coffee (favorably positioned relative to supply from Vietnam, which would be subject to a 46% tariff) and Chilean salmon (competing with supplies from Norway, which would be subject to a 16% tariff).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Global Economic Outlook: April 2025
data.description:
Trade tensions and related uncertainties point to continued turbulence in ﬁnancial markets. Corrections in equity prices were pronounced and widespread following the initial announcement of reciprocal tariffs on April 2. While the subsequent pause on April 9 led to strong initial rebounds, market conditions remain choppy, and risk aversion is elevated. Given growing concern over a possible waning of foreign appetite for US Treasurys, the jump in yields in early April was a worrying sign. While the acute near-term risk of a negative feedback loop between market turmoil and recession has diminished, the chronic problems of US policy volatility, related uncertainties and adverse economic spillovers are likely to linger.

We have cut our global growth forecasts for 2025 and 2026 in April’s update, reﬂecting the issues above. Our annual global real GDP growth forecast for 2025 has been lowered from 2.5% to 2.2%. Back in October 2024, prior to the US elections, our forecast was close to 3%. Next year’s global real GDP growth forecast has been reduced from 2.7% to 2.4%. In both years, projected global growth would be the weakest since the global ﬁnancial crisis of 2008–09, excluding the COVID-19 pandemic. Risks are to the downside.

Change in real GDP growth (%)

World

North America

Other Americas

Western Europe

Emerging Europe

Asia-Pacific

Mideast - North Africa

Sub-Saharan Africa

Data compiled April 15, 2025.

Source: S&P Global Market Intelligence.

© 2025 S&P Global.

April’s downward revisions to our growth forecasts are broad-based across economies. Our forecasts for annual US real GDP growth in 2025 and 2026 have been cut by 0.6 and 0.4 percentage point, respectively, to 1.3% and 1.5%. While a technical recession in the US is not our base case, it was looking like an increasingly close call prior to the pause on reciprocal tariffs. In Canada, we continue to forecast GDP contraction in mid-2025. In mainland China, as trade tensions with the US escalate, sub-4% annual growth rates are now projected this year and next. Our forecasts for growth in India, while still outperforming, have also been lowered. Western Europe’s near-stagnation is also forecast to continue in the near term, although ﬁscal policy should support eurozone growth from 2026, contributing to a modest global pickup.

The signals from S&P Global’s Purchasing Managers IndexTM data are not encouraging. Even prior to the chaos surrounding reciprocal tariffs, the global composite output index registered its weakest performance in ﬁve quarters during the ﬁrst quarter of 2025. Moreover, the surveys of business expectations have deteriorated across almost all major developed and emerging economies, with tariffs cited as the prime cause for concern. The price indexes for global manufacturing have also been picking up, with the steepest increases in input and output prices occurring in the US, again linked to tariffs. A silver lining amid the darkening clouds is the softening of services price indexes.

Consumer price inflation, annual change (%)

Data compiled April 15, 2025.

F = forecast.

Source: S&P Global Market Intelligence.

© 2025 S&P Global.

Our global and North American consumer price inﬂation forecasts for 2025 and 2026 were again revised upward in April. This continues the trend evident since late 2024, primarily reﬂecting shifts in US trade policy. The monthly consumer price inﬂation rate for core goods, which we calculate for the Group of Five (G5) economies, has been picking up since late 2024. Further increases are likely as the impact of rising tariffs becomes more widespread. In line with the PMI data, the downward trend in services inﬂation in the G5 economies has continued. February’s rate fell below 4% for the ﬁrst time since March 2022.

In our base case, we continue to expect limited easing by the US Federal Reserve this year. Our forecast remains for just one cut of 25 basis points in December, given the projected pickup in inﬂation in the coming months. A series of cuts are still forecast in 2026 as inﬂation worries recede. At the time of writing, futures markets discount about 80 basis points of Fed cuts this year, versus over 100 basis points at the height of the market turmoil. If the Fed cuts quicker than we expect, it will likely be owing to a rising recession risk, providing little comfort. Additional rate cuts in Western Europe are forecast to materialize more quickly than in the US. This is a key reason we expect the pace of recent currency appreciations versus the US dollar to moderate. Projected yen gains continue to reﬂect the expected divergence of monetary policy.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Japan Petroleum Exploration: Tough To Go Long On Oil (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Japan Petroleum Exploration (OTCPK:JPTXF) (JAPEX) continues to be one of the interesting low-EV propositions on the Japanese market, despite significant gains in share price. While the EV is low, we want to reiterate from previous coverage that the fact remains that much of the low EV comes from the fact that Inpex (OTCPK:IPXHY) is partially owned by JAPEX as part of a strategic relationship between the engineering firm and JAPEX's E&P businesses. Due to the strategic relationship, it is therefore unlikely that the company will ever execute plans to dispose of the shares, which are clearly not being considered by markets in the JAPEX equity bridge. The fact that JAPEX continues to stick to a low payout ratio will make this an enduring factor in the equity story until the company feels that it has reached a point where reinvestments aren't as critical.

While this is a negative factor in the story, the company has still shown a good drive to actually deploy cash, and has clearly telegraphed that cash balances are not to remain elevated. This has likely contributed to the stock performance in line with appreciation of low-EV plays in Japanese markets on corporate governance improvements. However, the more important factor for the company's latest stock price performance is going to be the state of oil prices. While a greater focus on oil export from the US possible under the Trump administration may support some of the activities of the company, in particular in the liquefaction and export business acquired through a stake in the Freeport LNG project, energy is down, including electricity which they also sell through their infrastructure division. It's an oil play after all and now is not the time for oil despite the valuation making JAPEX relatively compelling.

Comments

The E&P business grew in revenues on the back of volume increases from the Seagull project coming online and incrementally higher volumes from the tight oil development. Average prices were similar enough between the 9 months ending December between 2023 and 2024.

LNG volumes were up thanks to the inorganic impact of owning the liquefaction facility. Electricity was down and the gas volumes in Japan were flat. Revenues were up on account of higher natural gas prices.

E&P saw a decent performance thanks to incremental volumes (overseas in the Seagull project), despite pressure on oil prices that has only continued past the end of the close of books due to the economic concerns surrounding tariffs and trade wars, which has since brought oil lower. The IU segment is down on worse procurement margins as gas prices come up.

Bottom Line

What we want to focus on for a moment is the clear interest of the current shareholder base in the value story with JAPEX.

Including the Inpex holding gives the valuation as seen above. Assuming Inpex never becomes recognized in the equity bridge, which is a reasonable assumption, gives the valuation below. A reminder is that in past Q&As, the management has referred to the Inpex holding as a strategic holding, likely a basis for operational cooperation with Inpex. That means the company will likely not sell that Inpex holding.

The Q&A sessions continue to show promise. Management was grilled on the higher levels of capital coming from sales proceeds from some strategic sales, where shareholders wanted to confirm that management continues to have their eye on the ball. Management continues to telegraph that they don't intend for a burgeoning cash base and have been making investments into new projects, the latest of which being the Newport LNG project, with cash balances clearly falling since the beginning of the year. However, the company continues to target a 30% payout ratio. While the revisions as of February show management optimism with respect to crude prices, reality since February has shown that on the back of supply increases from OPEC+ and questionable demand, oil prices are not going to hold up.

This leads to the primary issue, which is the worries about the direction of oil markets. Forecasts may now be optimistic, and with an enduring supply pressure on crude prices, it's difficult to go long on any oil pick even with the JAPEX pullback and difficult to match value proposition on the back of a very safe balance sheet and equity bridge upside.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Sterling Infrastructure: Still A Decent Bet At The Current Multiple
data.description:
The Thesis

As Sterling Infrastructure (NASDAQ:STRL) exited 2024, its top line growth moderated due to a weakness in certain parts of the business. However, the company's core E-Infrastructure segment remains resilient and helped the company consolidate topline growth in the fourth quarter of 2024. I expect this segment to continue to deliver further as the data center end market and onshoring-related demand remain strong. This, along with a $1.7 billion backlog, should support overall top line growth in 2025. The company's margins are also expected to improve further with the company's focus on shifting its mix towards high-margin projects. Since my last bullish article on STRL, the stock is up about 3% despite the strong sell-off across the broader market during that period. Although the company's fundamentals and long-term prospects remain intact, I would still suggest buying this stock at the current multiple for the long term.

Last Quarter Highlights

Exiting the year, STRL delivered another quarter with positive growth across the top line, as its consolidated top line grew approximately 2.6% year-on-year to $498.8 million in the fourth quarter of 2024. This growth was primarily driven by strength in the company’s core segment, E-Infrastructure, which was up by about 7.6% year-on-year. This growth more than offset the impact of revenue decline in the company’s transportation and building solutions segment due to tougher comparisons from strong double-digit growth in Q4 last year. For the full year 2024, the consolidated top line was up by 7.3% year-on-year to $2.11 billion.

During the quarter, the company’s margins benefited from a favorable mix shift toward higher margins in large projects, primarily in the E-Infrastructure segment, as the company’s adjusted EBITDA expanded by 110 bps to 15.3%, as compared to the prior year. EBITDA growth also benefited the company’s bottom line performance as its adjusted EPS increased to $1.46 from $1.30 a year ago, beating the consensus estimates by about $0.13 in the fourth quarter of 2024.

Outlook

After a decent start to the year with high single-digit growth, the company’s top line growth gets slightly weakened as we move through the remaining quarters of 2024, with low single-digit growth in the fourth quarter. While this slowing top line was primarily a result of revenue declines in the company’s transportation and building solutions business, the company’s core segment, E-Infrastructure, continues to see favorable growth. I expect this momentum to continue further into 2025, as the demand environment for the company’s solutions remains robust, primarily in the data center end market, which grew over 50% year-on-year in the last quarter.

In addition to data centers, the company’s E-Infrastructure segment also continues to see healthy order and volume growth from the onshoring of manufacturing activities, which, along with the strengthening environment in the e-commerce and small warehouse market, should support the segment growth in the quarters ahead. The company’s backlog levels also remain elevated at nearly $1.7 billion as of the fourth quarter of 2024, of which nearly $1 billion is in the E-Infrastructure segment, up by 27% year-on-year. In my view, while the overall demand environment looks good, this backlog should also support top line growth in 2025 and beyond.

Apart from these, the company continues to shift toward large, multi-phase critical projects for greater visibility of future phases of work. So far in 2025, the company has won awards worth several hundred million dollars for E-Infrastructure projects, which are not included in the $1 billion backlog. Also, the discussion is going on with customers for such large projects, which are expected to start from 2027 onwards. In my view, these activities in the company’s core business indicate continued momentum even beyond 2025, positioning STRL to continue its growth in the longer term.

Talking about the company’s transportation segment, it also continues to see healthy demand in the Rocky Mountain regions and Arizona markets. While these markets are expected to continue to drive growth in 2025, the segment has built a backlog for over two years already. Bid activity also remains good for future awards, which, along with the benefit from the second cycle of federal funding, should further support the segment's growth in the quarters ahead.

However, the company’s building solution business is expected to continue to face headwinds from softness in the overall housing market, driven by affordability challenges. Although this might impact the segment's performance in the short term, demographic tailwinds from population growth in key geographies of Dallas, Houston, Phoenix, and Fort Worth should support demand growth with the need for new homes in the region in the coming years. In my view, there is still significant room for opportunity to grow market share in these regions, which should benefit the company’s building solution revenue in the longer term.

Overall, while the short-term outlook appears to be good with a continued healthy demand environment in most parts of the business and strong backlog levels, benefits from rising investment in data center infrastructure and the company’s continued focus on winning large projects should drive demand for the company in the future. In addition to this, the company remains focused on strategic acquisitions to expand its footprint and offerings further. The company’s financials also remain sound, with strong liquidity and a net leverage well below the target range, which, along with credit facilities, should help it in future potential acquisitions, further supporting the company in its top line growth in the coming years.

Valuation Update

I covered STRL earlier this year in February with a bullish view, giving this stock a buy rating, primarily due to its favorable growth prospects and improved valuation post the price drop in January. Since then, the stock is up in single digits, against a nearly 10% drop in the S&P 500 amid tariff-driven uncertainty across the broader market. While the single-digit increase appears to be less, it looks really impressive considering the brutal sell-off that happened across Wall Street in the last couple of weeks.

Currently, the company's stock is trading at a non-GAAP forward P/E ratio of 16.85, based on FY25 EPS estimates of $8.03. Although the stock still looks slightly expensive when compared to its historical multiple of 14.48x, its strong forward double-digit growth rate, significantly above its sector median as can be seen in the table above, still makes it look attractive. Going ahead, I expect the company's top line to continue its growth in 2025 with a continued healthy demand environment and robust backlog level. STRL is also focusing on shifting the mix toward high-margin large projects, which, along with the benefits from operational improvement, should continue to support margins and bottom-line growth in the quarter ahead, further enhancing the stock valuation in the future.

Risks

Similar to my previous article, the key risk for STRL remains the ongoing softness in its business solutions segment due to the weak housing market in the region. Although the anticipated rate cuts in 2025 are expected to drive growth across the housing market, it remains uncertain. In my view, if this weakness continues further, then despite the segment being smaller in size, the company's overall performance might be impacted, potentially impacting the stock performance in the future.

Final Takeaway And Rating

As discussed above, the company's stock is trading slightly above its historical multiple. The company's top line is expected to benefit from healthy demand for its offerings and strong backlog in the quarters ahead. This, along with the benefit from a focus on winning higher margin projects and increasing operational efficiency, should drive margin growth in 2025 and beyond. Although the long-term prospects also look favorable with rising investments in the data center end market and a robust project pipeline across the segment, I believe that the current valuation presents a decent opportunity to invest in this company. Hence, I would like to maintain my BUY rating on this stock at the current levels.

data.title: T-Rex 2X Long MSTR Daily Target ETF: Leverage On Leverage
data.description:
MSTU Overview

T-Rex 2X Long MSTR Daily Target ETF (BATS:MSTU) is an ETF that aims to produce 2x the daily return of Strategy (MSTR), formerly known as MicroStrategy which is effectively a Bitcoin holding company. As such, an analysis of MSTU requires an assessment of MSTR, which by extension requires an assessment of Bitcoin.

Current MSTR Premium to Underlying Net Asset Value is 126%

See below for a breakdown of Net Asset, including number of BTC multiplied by current market price, cash on hand, and debt numbers, with no intangibles.

Market Value

Cash & Restricted Cash

$0.04 Bn

Bitcoin (478,740 BTC)

$40.58 Bn

Total Market Value of Assets

$40.62 Bn

Convertible Debt

$7.18 Bn

Net Asset Value (NAV) FY24

$33.44 Bn

YTD 25 Convertible Debt

$0.95 Bn

YTD 25 Newly Purchased (49,445)

$4.19 Bn

Preferred Stock STRK

$0.594 Bn

Preferred Stock STRF

$0.71 Bn

Assets Added YTD

$4.19 Bn

Debt + Preferred Stock Added YTD

$2.254 Bn

New NAV YTD

$1.93 Bn

Updated NAV as 4/14/2025

$35.37 Bn

Number of shares outstanding

266,177,509

NAV Per Share

$132.88

Source: Strategy 8-K, 10-K, and Author's Calculations

MSTR Stock is currently trading at a premium of around 126%. The current stock price of $299.98 implies that investors are paying $191,501 per Bitcoin for exposure via MSTR stock. In other words, Bitcoin would need to go up to $191,501 in order for Strategy's Net Asset Value to justify its current stock price. This premium may have initially originated due to MSTR being the only way for investors to get exposure to Bitcoin through a brokerage account. However, the Bitcoin ETFs now offer a method to get exposure to Bitcoin through a brokerage account without paying any sort of premium, in exchange for a very small annual management fee. In the case of IBIT, the management fee is just 0.25%.

Premium is High (or Low) Relative to Historical Premium

The Strategy Tracker website lists historical MSTR premiums against its Bitcoin holdings. The premiums are slightly lower in the graph compared to reality as they do not have an option to include its convertible debt. However, it is still directionally useful to look at.

The premium listed on Strategy Tracker's website peaked on November 20, 2024, at 3.9x or 290%, and bottomed on March 2, 2025, at 1.6x or 60%. Due to ATM Stock Issuance seen in 2025 YTD, the current premium as listed on Strategy Tracker is just 2x or 100%, decreasing by about 19,000 basis points in the period from Nov 2024 to April 2025.

In my view, getting back to the NAV premium peak of 3.9x would be almost impossible in the short to medium term as MSTR keeps issuing new shares even at the current premium. While on its face it appears that there's an opportunity to trade a swing in the premium since we are close to its historical bottom, there doesn't appear to be a fundamental reason for the premium to rise again given the heavy use of the ATM facility causing sell pressure.

Financing Strategy Was Effective but No Longer Looks Effective

MSTR's financing strategy rests on 3 prongs. The main one is the ATM facility (common stock issuance) which is accretive to NAV but suppresses the premium. MSTR stock is generally correlated to Bitcoin, but it has a premium it can arbitrage through common stock issuance. When it leverages that premium to finance BTC purchases, it consequently increases the NAV per share because the proceeds from issuing each overvalued share outweighs the dilution effect on NAV of adding an additional share. However, increases in NAV per share are not correlated to increases in stock price in the short or medium term because the common share issuances cause immediate large sell pressure.

The second prong is convertible debt issuances, which appear to be highly effective. MSTR issues 0–1% interest bonds convertible into equity at a sizable premium to the current stock price. For example, the last issuance was in February 2025, when MSTR issued a convertible note for $2 Billion with a maturity date of March 2030 at 0% interest rate and with a conversion price of $433, which is 33.6% above the price of $324 at the date of issuance.

However, it seems that MSTR has recently switched over to issuance of preferred stock with high dividend rates, which is a sign that they are now both unable to effectively capitalize on common stock issuances without negatively affecting stock price, and unable to issue much more debt at favorable interest rates.

MSTR has issued 2 sets of preferred stock, with the first issuance at an effective 10% dividend rate with an 8% stated dividend yield on $100 face value, but issued at an $80 price. The second issuance was at an effective dividend rate of 11.76%, due to a 10% stated dividend rate on a $100 face value issued at $85. See here for the offering details.

Clear Bitcoin Cyclicality Appears Bullish for MSTR NAV and MSTU

Bitcoin has been moving in 4-year cycles, with 3 years up followed by 1 year down. This has happened 3 times in Bitcoin's history, but we are now at a crossroads for cycle 4. See below for historical annual returns so you can see the clear pattern.

Year

Bitcoin ROI

2011

1467%

2012

187%

2013

5870%

2014

-61%

2015

35%

2016

124%

2017

1,338%

2018

-73%

2019

94%

2020

302%

2021

60%

2022

-64%

2023

156%

2024

121%

2025 (YTD)

-14%

Source

However, we are now at a crossroads, with this year being the post-halving year, which is historically bullish for Bitcoin. However, we are at a negative 14% return year to date for Bitcoin, and if Bitcoin does not recover by year-end, then this year may be the end of the 4-year cycle. If the cycle continues, then Bitcoin will recover and end the year substantially positive, which would generate strong NAV returns for MSTR.

However, this does not necessarily result in stock appreciation for MSTR and subsequently may not result in appreciation of MSTU. For example, let's compare the MSTR peak in the last cycle with Bitcoin's peak in the last cycle.

When MTSR peaked in 2021, its price reached its previous all-time high closing price of $103.4 in February 2021. Meanwhile, Bitcoin was around $55,000. Bitcoin then went up about 20% to $69,000 in October 2021, while MSTR went down about 19% over the same period to about $81.

This shows that being bullish on Bitcoin is not enough to be bullish on MSTR or MSTU, as the correlation between the two became negative in the previous cycle in the post-halving year.

MSTU Volatility Decay

MSTU aims to achieve a 2x levered return against MSTR over daily time frames. However, volatility decay may erode returns due to big volatility swings.

For example, if MSTR's price was trading at $100, and had a meltdown, losing 10%, and then recovered by 11%, it would trade back at $100 again.

MSTU, being 2x daily levered, would lose 20% of its value the first day, and then recover by 22% on the next day. However, it would then only trade back at $97.6 instead of $100. This means that volatility can cause MSTU to decline even while MSTR stays at the same price. Keep in mind that MSTR is highly volatile due to its Bitcoin holdings, and frequently has large daily swings. This makes an investment in MSTU much more risky than MSTR.

While MSTU is supposed to achieve 2x levered return against MSTR in the short term, which can be more favorable in a consistent MSTR uptrend and when volatility is low, in a highly volatile market it could be destructive to invest in it as the leverage could cause losses even without MSTR going down.

Of course, leverage also goes both ways, and if MSTR goes down then an investment in MSTU will fall twice as hard.

MSTU is Not a Buy Due to Large MSTR Premium, Unclear Correlation With BTC Over Next 6–8 Months, and MSTU Volatility Decay

While we are bullish on Bitcoin over the next 6–8 months, it is not clear that this will translate into gains for MSTR or MSTU. MSTR's large 126% premium to underlying NAV is too high to strongly recommend a buy. We like to buy things below liquidation value, but occasionally, it can make sense to buy things that are overvalued if there's a clear signal it may become even more overvalued. However, MSTR has been aggressively tapping the ATM over the past few months, which is preventing the premium from rising back to historical levels. As such, there is no clear signal that the premium will increase in the short or medium term.

Looking at cyclicality, MSTR peaked in February 2021 of the last cycle while BTC peaked in October 2025, so it's not clear that Bitcoin rising further would result in MSTR or MSTU increasing further.

Additionally, with the recent market volatility due to tariff news and retaliations which is still unresolved, MSTU volatility decay may cause MSTU to capture upside at a less than 2x rate, making it not worth taking on the additional risk versus buying MSTR.

As such, we have a sell rating on MSTU due to MSTR premium, sell pressure from ATM facility, unclear correlation with BTC, and volatility decay.

data.title: F.N.B. Corporation 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by F.N.B. Corporation in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: The Trade Desk: Firesale Overdone, Now A GARP Gem With Technical Support
data.description:
Momentum came crashing down in February, and that coincided with a sharp decline in shares of The Trade Desk (NASDAQ:TTD). While the momentum factor has actually rebounded in the past month—losing just 2.4% since mid-March, outperforming the S&P 500—TTD has not been so fortunate.

Ahead of its Q1 reporting date in May, I have a buy rating on the left-for-dead $24 billion market cap Advertising industry stock within the Communication Services sector. The valuation is now attractive, while the technicals point to a potential bottom, with long-term support in play. Seasonality is also strong now through early Q3.

Momentum Factor Off the Mat

Back in February, TTD reported a mixed set of quarterly results. Q4 non-GAAP EPS summed to $0.59, which was actually a two-cent beat. Revenue came in at $741 million, up 22% from the same period a year earlier, but that was an $18 million miss. The company announced an additional share repurchase program, bringing the total tally of future buybacks to $1 billion, but that did little to keep the bears at bay.

Shares plunged 33% in the session that followed—among the worst reactions to a Q4 report in the market over the past reporting season. Looking ahead, the options market prices in another wild ride after the Q1 update—the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the May 7 earnings date assumes a 13.2% stock price swing.

Looking closer at the previous quarter, it was a disappointing Q4—BofA noted that it was the first quarter in 33 that is missed on both the top line and with its EBITDA, Wall Street consensus target. Company-specific problems plagued TTD, which had been soaring heading into the February print. Margins dropped, and the outlook was weak. Still, The Trade Desk commands a strong presence in high-growth areas like connected TV (CTV), retail media, and high-growth ex-US geographies.

A company reorg is ongoing, though, so we could see growing pains—shares appear to be pricing that in. The management team was upbeat on the call, noting its +26% YoY revenue growth and mid-high teens top-line growth for the quarter that just ended.

On the earnings outlook, there has obviously been a barrage of sell-side EPS downgrades. Still, the per-share earnings growth rate is decent for the balance of FY 2025, and we're looking at bottom-line growth in the low-mid-20% range. All the while, profitability trends are very strong, and free cash flow per share is solid at $1.29, resulting in a FCF yield near 3%.

The Trade Desk: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, TTD looks much more attractive today. The forward non-GAAP price-to-earnings multiple is now under 30x—an all-time low going back to its 2017 IPO. If EPS forecasts come anywhere close to reality, then the stock is very cheap. Let's assume $1.90 of operating EPS over the next year and apply just a 33x P/E.

That yields a fair value of $63, about $15 above today's stock price. A 33x P/E would be a normalized PEG ratio of just 1.5x based on CY 26–27 EPS growth rates. For perspective, its historical PEG is almost 3. Shares also trade at an incredible discount to the long-term price-to-sales ratio.

TTD: Cheap PEG Ratio, Discounted Heavily on a P/S Basis

TTD P/E Sinks to 27x, The Lowest Since the IPO

TTD is a high-risk play, as evidenced by the February plunge. The significant restructuring announced in the Q4 report brings about heightened uncertainty. Moreover, industry competition is fierce—Google and Amazon could take away from the company's share, or at least lead to softer industry margins. Additional unknowns may stem from the regulatory front and any missteps on data privacy with its customers.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show an unconfirmed Q1 2025 earnings date of Wednesday, May 7 AMC. Later in the month, the company hosts its Annual General Meeting on May 27.

Corporate Event Calendar

The Technical Take

TTD trades with a whopping 86% implied volatility percentage with the earnings date in sight. Notice in the chart below that shares are now down 65% from the late 2024 all-time high of $141. The long-term 200-day moving average has rolled over and is more than double the current stock price. TTD hit technical oversold conditions earlier this month, but there are some positive signs to note.

First, take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph—there's a bullish momentum divergence to price. That's a potential harbinger of better price action to come. Furthermore, TTD has fallen all the way back to its 2022–2023 lows near the $40 mark. So, long here with a stop under, say $38, would make for a favorable risk/reward entry.

Unfortunately, there's now a high amount of volume by price, up to $90. I also spotted that horizontal resistance is right about there, along with the old uptrend support line. So, technically, we could be looking at $40 of upside. Getting there may take time, given the supply of shares/congestion from the past four years, and $60-$62 may be near-term resistance.

TTD: Shares Approach Long-Term Support Near $40, Bullish RSI Divergence

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on The Trade Desk. I see this growth stock as attractive on valuation when considering its growth potential, while the chart presents a favorable risk/reward chance ahead of the Q1 report next month.

data.title: Bulls In A China Shop: 5 Chinese Stocks To Ride Out The Trade War
data.description:
Has China Taken the Upper Hand in Trump’s Trade War?

As Washington and Beijing remain locked in a power struggle over tariffs, Donald Trump has maintained that his decision to exempt certain high-tech Chinese exports from reciprocal tariffs was by no means a sign of surrender to Xi Jinping’s government.

"NOBODY is getting ‘off the hook’ for the unfair Trade Balances, and Non-Monetary Tariff Barriers, that other Countries have used against us, especially not China which, by far, treats us the worst! There was no Tariff "exception" announced on Friday," Trump wrote on his Social Truth platform.

Trump’s 145% tariffs on China will undoubtedly inflict major pain. Approximately 15% of the country’s exports went to the U.S. last year, amounting to a trade surplus of nearly $300B. However, China holds one major advantage in its fast-escalating trade war with the U.S.: China can more easily replace its imports from the U.S. than vice versa.

An analysis by The Financial Times of data from the Harvard Atlas of Economic Complexity showed that US exports to China were more agriculture-focused, including soybeans, cotton, beef and poultry. Most U.S. imports from China were more heavily composed of electronics, technology, and processed minerals.

While China will suffer a hit from Trump’s tariffs (the country relies on the U.S. for key semiconductor components and pharmaceuticals, to name a few), its exports are harder to replicate Stateside. China has access to rare minerals such as dysprosium and terbium, essential manufacturing components for electric vehicles. Reducing the U.S.’s dependence on China-made electronics and machinery, while not impossible, would be a long and arduous process.

“U.S. dependence on China is higher because China can source agricultural products from elsewhere more easily than the U.S. can replace electronics and machinery,” Marta Bengoa, professor of international economics at the City University of New York, told the FT. “Beijing is already buying up soybeans from Brazil, for example, so in the end China has a bit more leverage.”

Meanwhile, a weakening U.S. dollar has strengthened China’s economic prowess and made it more expensive for the U.S. to import its goods while also pushing American investors into foreign markets. Data released this week revealed that China’s export growth surged to a four-month high in March—far above expectations—illustrating the dominance of Chinese exports and ramping up bullishness on China despite tariff headwinds.

How the trade war pans out between the two nations is still anyone’s guess, as Trump and Xi continue to retaliate against the other’s ever-changing demands. From an economic perspective, however, it appears that China has the tools to come out of this trade war in a better position than the U.S.—though neither will emerge unscathed.

China’s ‘Biggest Losers’ Primed for Big Wins

The sell-off triggered by tariffs has shaken markets across the globe, and China is no exception. Some of the country’s top tech, finance, consumer, and healthcare companies have suffered significant hits as trade tensions between the U.S. and China have boiled over, despite strong financial foundations and sector-leading performance.

The widespread volatility is enough to unnerve even the most seasoned investors. But, those who resist panic-selling and remain steadfast in their investment strategies are typically rewarded with higher long-term gains. Even better, a downmarket can present strategic opportunities to invest in beaten-down stocks poised for strong returns once the storm passes.

At the end of last month, I laid out my top 5 ‘Biggest Losers’ worth buying the dip on, all of which have fantastic potential for a resurgence despite recent declines. In this article, I’ll discuss five top Chinese stocks that the market has similarly pummeled despite their strong underlying fundamentals. Each selection boasts a Quant Strong Buy rating, powerful momentum and growth outlook, and a discounted valuation—all of which leave it well-positioned for a potential comeback once volatility subsides.

You may be familiar with the adage, “Fear creates losses.” The precept highlights how panic selling during market downturns can lead to financial setbacks. Since 2010, the market has pulled back five times by more than 15% (July 1, 2010; August 8, 2011; December 20, 2018; March 9, 2020; and May 9, 2022). Investors who purchased the top 10 Quant Strong Buy stocks during these 15% downturns and held them for 2 years saw 117% gains on average vs. the S&P 500’s 50% return. SA Quant Strong Buys are based on backtesting and not actual recommendations at the time and subsequent performance results.

SA Quant Strong Buys Surged An Avg. 117% Following 15% Market Corrections

Despite being some of the Chinese market’s ‘Biggest Losers,’ my five selections detailed below are also benefiting from powerful industrial tailwinds—leading innovations in AI, healthcare, fintech, and more—despite being dragged down by widespread investor panic. Over the past month, these stocks posted an average return of -18% vs. the S&P 500’s -5.75%, but remain 'Strong Buys' due to their strong fundamentals and sector-leading performance.

China’s ‘Biggest Losers’

Each stock was selected using Seeking Alpha’s Quant System. This method combines powerful computer processing with our special ‘Quantamental’ analysis, designed to remove emotion from the investment process. The SA algorithm separates the weak companies from the strong ones based on their historical financials, analysts' consensus growth estimates, and hundreds of financial metrics. Using these insights, it ranks them by valuation, growth, profitability, momentum, and EPS revisions, assigning letter grades to each category to help contextualize thousands of data points.

Let’s dive into my top five ‘Biggest Losers’ with big potential.

1. LexinFintech Holdings Ltd. (NASDAQ: LX)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.2B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/16/25): 1 out of 685

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/16/25): 1 out of 39

Ranked first among all financials and consumer finance stocks, LexinFintech Holdings Ltd. is a Chinese consumer finance platform and e-commerce business. The company offers customers personal loans to purchase items on its e-commerce site Fenquile, which carries everything from electronics to clothing to household essentials. Users can also make purchases outside Fenquile by linking LX’s loans to other payment providers, including Alibaba's Alipay. The Shenzhen-based group posted strong Q4 2024 results, with a 17% quarterly increase in net income—its fourth consecutive quarter of improved profitability—and a 2% quarterly increase in loan origination to RMB $52B, or $7.02B. It declared a $0.11 dividend shortly after.

LX has shed around 30.5% over the past month amid the recent global sell-off and the escalation of the U.S.-China trade war since Trump’s ‘Liberation Day.’ However, its strong fundamentals—backed by ‘A’ Quant Factor Grades across the board—underline its potential for long-term growth despite geopolitical pressures.

LX Stock’s Quant Breakdown

LX’s ‘A-’ valuation is backed by a fantastic forward non-GAAP price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio of 4.13, a 57.4% discount from the 9.7 sector median. A forward price-to-sales ratio of 0.57, a 76% discount, emphasizes the low price of its shares relative to its potential sales growth as it continues to improve its suite of lending opportunities.

Growth is equally strong with forward EBITDA growth of 31.7%, a 183% difference from the sector, and long-term earnings per share (EPS) growth of 43.28%—a 358% difference vs. the 9.4% sector median. Profitability has improved from a ‘B-’ to an ‘A-’ over the past three months, as evidenced by its latest earnings results. Its momentum has remained strong during the recent downturn, with a one-year price performance of 310%—a 3,217% difference to the 9.3% sector median.

Like LX, my next pick continues to dominate the Quant rankings in its sector despite the ongoing trade war.

2. Kingsoft Cloud Holdings Limited (NASDAQ: KC)

  • Market Capitalization: $3.2B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/16/25): 1 out of 539

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/16/25): 1 out of 22

The reigning Quant champ of the technology sector as well as in the internet services and infrastructure industry, Kingsoft Cloud Holdings Limited, is a Chinese cloud services provider offering infrastructure as a service ((IaaS)) infrastructure, platform as a service (PaaS) middleware, and software as a service (SaaS) applications. The Beijing-based company returned a whopping 385% over the past six months, even though it lost around 25% over the past 30 days amid rising trade tensions. The group has invested heavily in AI, broadening its customer base and engaging in a strategic partnership with Chinese electronic giant Xiaomi (OTCPK:XIACF) as it rises to take on China’s internet giants as an independent player. As SA analyst The Value Pendulum noted earlier this year:

Goldman Sachs (GS) projected that KC's AI revenue contribution as a percentage of its top line could rise to 26% and 31% in 2025 and 2026…KC's overall top-line performance is likely to be even better in the future, taking into account the positive AI revenue growth momentum.

Those efforts have already begun to pay off. The group reported Q4 GAAP EPS of -$0.01, beating expectations by $0.10. Total revenue growth doubled year-over-year to 30%, boosted by 34% Y/Y revenue growth of its public cloud business and 23% Y/Y revenue growth of its enterprise cloud revenue.

KC Stock’s Quant Breakdown

Forward revenue growth of 15.8%, an impressive 137% difference from the sector median, reflects KC’s strong AI tailwinds and improving profitability despite the ongoing trade war. “This quarter, we achieved both revenue growth and profitability improvement, marking a turnaround from the company's longstanding history of operating losses and marching towards the goal of net profit breakeven,” Vice Chairman and CEO Tao Zou told investors last month. KC’s valuation remains on the higher side, likely a result of the Chinese government’s increasing emphasis on AI following the successful rise of DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK), which catapulted its share price. Profitability has slowly improved over the past six months, rising from a ‘D-’ to a ‘D.’ Its scaled-up AI investments are reflected by an ‘A+’ CAPEX/sales ratio (TTM) of 53%, a 2,364.2% difference from the median.

My next selection hails from the world of e-commerce, but has demonstrated similar resilience to tariffs and trade tensions by doubling down on domestic growth.

3. JD.com, Inc. (NASDAQ: JD)

  • Market Capitalization: $54.3B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/16/25): 5 out of 479

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/16/25): 1 out of 27

Headquartered in Beijing, China, JD.com, Inc. is the must-needed supply chain-based technology and logistics company offering various products and marketplace services. It develops, owns, and manages logistics facilities. The Beijing-based group is reportedly setting up a $27.B fund to boost the domestic sales of China’s top exporters as the U.S.-China trade war heats up, helping it make SA’s list of top China stocks. Despite losing 15% over the past month, it has returned over 8% year-to-date and nearly 48% over the past year—reflecting its remaining upside potential.

The company has a healthy balance sheet with over $7.5B in cash, significant year-over-year cash flow growth, and an attractive valuation that overshadows trade-related headwinds. As SA analyst Kevin George recently wrote:

The company's current valuation is far too low compared to the underlying trends, and I believe it is a good time to invest. Trump's tariffs could lead to a boost in domestic consumption in China, and we may also be seeing a ‘peak in U.S. exceptionalism,’ according to BofA analysts. That puts China in a better position to grab investment flows if the economy can get a boost.

JD Stock’s Quant Breakdown

JD touts an attractive valuation compared to many of its tech peers, in the U.S. and China, which has grown cheaper in response to its falling shares. Its forward price-to-earnings growth (PEG) ratio comes at a more than 20% discount to the sector median, while its forward non-GAAP P/E of 8.08 represents a sector-leading 41% discount.

Growth remains solid, with forward EPS diluted growth of 18.3%, a 196% difference to the 6.2% sector median, and long-term EPS growth of 28%, a 268% difference. Momentum continues to dominate the wider consumer discretionary sector as the trade war has weighed on e-commerce and other major Chinese exporters, with a three-month price performance of nearly 10% compared to the -16.3% median.

Like JD, my next pick is finding new and innovative ways to press forward amid mounting tariffs.

4. Qifu Technology, Inc. (NASDAQ: QFIN)

  • Market Capitalization: $5.4B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/16/25): 6 out of 685

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/16/25): 2 out of 39

Qifu Technology, Inc. is an AI-powered credit platform operating under the Qifu Jietiao brand in China. The company’s stock surged last month after it increased its semi-annual dividend by 17%, posted better-than-expected Q4 earnings, and issued strong 2025 guidance. That better-than-expected performance has helped QFIN remain in the green on a YTD basis despite losing around 15% over the past month. China recently unveiled a “special action plan” to boost domestic spending. The increased economic stimulus, paired with continued improvements in QFIN’s AI capabilities and user acquisition, could give it the boost it needs to emerge from the trade war unscathed.

As SA analyst Astrada Advisors recently wrote:

We believe that leveraging AI will be key in driving growth on both the supply and demand sides. QFIN has already pivoted towards this path to improve operational efficiency, which, we believe, will translate to financial results this year.

QFIN Stock’s Quant Breakdown

QFIN’s valuation stands out amongst its financial peers. Its forward non-GAAP P/E of 5.42 represents a more than 44% discount to the 9.69 sector median. Its ‘A+’ forward PEG of 0.39 is even more attractive, at a 66% discount to the 1.4 sector median.

QFIN’s increased spending on AI and domestic growth tailwinds from the Chinese government are reflected by forward EPS GAAP growth of 30.3%, a 221% difference to the sector median, and forward EBITDA growth of 21.6%, a 92.6% difference. Profitability has improved from a ‘B+’ to an ‘A-’ over the past three months, as reflected by a return on total assets (TTM) of 13%, a 1,025.9% difference from the sector. With seven positive EPS revisions over the past 90 days and zero negative, analysts agree that QFIN’s growth will likely keep revving up despite trade obstacles.

5. Zai Lab Limited (NASDAQ: ZLAB)

  • Market Capitalization: $3.4B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/16/25): 19 out of 1007

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/16/25): 9 out of 497

Zai Lab Limited is a biopharmaceutical company focused on discovering, developing, and marketing treatments across oncology, immunology, neuroscience, and infectious diseases. Its top drugs include Zejula, a cure for ovarian cancer, and NUZYRA, a treatment for skin infections. Despite losing around 20% over the past month, the Shanghai-based group’s powerful revenue growth and groundbreaking medical treatments have helped keep momentum strong amid growing trade tensions.

The company aims to achieve $2bn in annual revenues by 2028, a goal backed by strong financial performance with double-digit topline growth in 2024.

The drugmaker manufactures many of its treatments in the U.S. and Europe for local distribution, which could help shield it from tariffs. As SA analyst Myriam Alvarez recently noted on some of its top products:

ZLAB should be safe from these tariffs due to their manufacturing structure. Also, Vyvgart’s ongoing commercialization efforts are likely going to keep paying off, so it should further derisk it even if trade war tensions continue ramping up. You see, ZLAB actually manufactures and commercializes this particular drug locally in China, so in that sense, it should be relatively insulated from tariffs.

ZLAB Stock’s Quant Breakdown

ZLAB’s valuation has remained at a 'D+' as its stock has soared 114% over the past year, but we could soon see its share price come down due to trade pressures—even as growth and momentum stay elevated. Powerful growth tailwinds are reflected by forward revenue growth of 46.6%, a 498% difference to the 7.8% sector median. Excellent momentum is reflected by a three-month price performance of nearly 30%, a significant difference from the -19.4% median shared by the healthcare sector.

With six positive EPS revisions over the past 90 days and one negative, analysts broadly agree that temporary trade war woes will not permanently weigh on ZLAB’s performance. ZLAB, like the other four Chinese stocks discussed in this piece, continues to demonstrate resilience in the face of a fast-escalating trade war—a testament to its strong financials and ‘Strong Buy’ ratings.

Conclusion

Like many U.S. counterparts, China’s top-performing companies have been hammered by tariffs and trade war tensions over the past month, despite their strong balance sheets and sector-leading Quant Factor Grades. But the market always returns to fundamentals. All five selections in this piece have the necessary ingredients to ride out the tariff war despite temporary declines.

During periods of uncertainty and market volatility, I’ve often written and frequently emphasized the value of a barbell investing approach, combining fundamentally strong, opportunistically mispriced stocks with value-driven, dividend-paying equities to balance risk and enhance stability in volatile markets. Striking that balance requires removing emotion from the process and thinking critically through each decision. Before succumbing to nerves, utilize SA’s Quant System to examine your holdings' underlying metrics and Quant Factor Grades. Sometimes, you may find that the biggest losers still carry the biggest potential.

data.title: Zurn Elkay Water Solutions: Nice Growth, But Not At A Nice Price
data.description:
My search for interesting companies has brought me once again to another intriguing business. This is none other than Zurn Elkay Water Solutions Corporation (NYSE:ZWS), a company that management describes as a growth oriented, pure play water management firm that offers up a portfolio of specification-driven water management solutions. Over the past few years, this enterprise has seen revenue grow nicely. Profits and cash flows have followed a similar trajectory. Moving forward, management expects continued growth. However, a company like this does not come cheap. I certainly would not say that shares are expensive. Having said that, I would say that they are fairly valued at this time.

Of course, it is important to be flexible as new data comes into the picture. And it just so happens that we will be seeing some new data shortly. That is because, after the market closes on April 22nd, management will be reporting financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Leading up to that point in time, analysts expect continued revenue and profit growth. Unless the company can really wow me, however, I don't think that would be enough to justify a more bullish assessment than the ‘hold’ I have decided to rate it for now.

Taking a sip of Zurn Elkay Water Solutions

If my initial description of Zurn Elkay Water Solutions is nebulous, it's only because management's initial description of the business is that way. To really understand the firm, it would be helpful if we dig deeper into it. For starters, the company has a portfolio of professional grade water safety and control products, flow systems products, hygienic and environmental products, and filtered drinking water products that are used for both the public and private aspects of society. The goal here is to improve the health and hydration of its customers while also promoting human safety and environmental care.

Through major brands such as Zurn, Elkay, Wilkins, Green Turtle, World Dryer, and more, the company caters to a wide array of customers. On the institutional construction side, for instance, it works with players in education, the healthcare industry, and government agencies. The commercial construction end users for the enterprise consist of retailers, accommodations firms, restaurants, warehouses, sports arenas, office property owners, and more. It also caters to the waterworks and market by selling its products to municipal water and wastewater entities, and also those that deal in the transportation space.

Over the past few years, management has used its roughly 1,100 independent sales representatives spread across 180 different agencies to sell its products in around 30 countries. The results have been phenomenal during this time. From 2022 through 2024, revenue has shot up from $1.28 billion to $1.57 billion. Most of this growth occurred from 2022 to 2023 when revenue jumped an impressive 19.4%. This rise was because of a major merger that the company completed that saw Zurn Water Solutions Corporation combine with Elkay Manufacturing Company. But growth is growth all the same. The more modest increase in sales from 2023 to 2024, about 2.4% in total, was thanks to core sales growth of about 3%, which management claimed was caused by growth across almost all of its product categories. But beyond that, management didn't really provide much in the way of detail here.

On the bottom line, the picture has been quite nice. Over the last three years, net income has skyrocketed from $61.7 million to $160.2 million. Even on an adjusted basis, which excludes things like restructuring expenses and the amortization of intangible assets, net profits would have risen from $144.3 million to $223.9 million. Operating cash flow approximately tripled from $97 million to $293.5 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get an approximate doubling from $144.9 million to $283.9 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $264.6 million to $390.4 million.

Management expects continued growth to occur this year. Even though the commercial market should see a reduction in revenue at a rate that will be in the low single digit range, and even though the residential market will be essentially flat, there should be growth associated with the institutional market and the waterworks market. This core sales growth that should be in the low single digit range should allow EBITDA this year to be between $405 million and $420 million. Given this and the other guidance provided by management, we should anticipate net profits of around $265.6 million and adjusted operating cash flow of about $300 million.

If we use these estimates and the historical results from last year, we can see in the chart above how the stock is currently priced. While the price to earnings multiple for 2024 is a bit on the lofty side, all of the other trading multiples for the company are in a range that I would consider to be fair value. Relative to similar companies, shares also seem to be in the fair value range. In the table below, I compared it with five such enterprises. On both a price to earnings basis and on an EV to EBITDA basis, I found that three of the five companies were cheaper than it is. This number does drop to one of the five when using the price to operating cash flow approach.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Zurn Elkay Water Solutions 22.7 17.9 13.8
CSW Industrials (CSWI) 33.7 27.6 21.5
Armstrong World Industries (AWI) 22.0 21.9 12.9
Trex Company (TREX) 26.4 41.4 17.6
Simpson Manufacturing (SSD) 19.4 18.5 12.5

As I mentioned at the start of this article, management will be reporting financial results for the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year after the market closes on April 22nd. Leading up to that point, analysts believe that revenue will be around $383.6 million. Should this come to fruition, it would be 2.6% above the $373.8 million the company reported the previous year. They also expect earnings per share to be around $0.22. That would be up from the $0.19 per share reported for the first quarter of 2024 and would translate to a rise in net profits from $34.3 million to $38 million.

On an adjusted basis, the current expectation is for earnings of $0.29 per share. That would match what the company achieved last year and would mean adjusted net profits of $50.2 million. In the table above, you can also see other profitability metrics for the first quarter of 2024. Even though we don't have estimates provided by analysts for these, I would wager that a rise in revenue and profits would likely translate to a rise in these metrics as well.

One other thing that I would like to touch on is that Zurn Elkay Water Solutions has some other positive attributes to it. For starters, as you can see by looking at the image above, the net leverage ratio for the company has come down over the last couple of years. This was aided by the merger that allowed a rise in revenue, profits, and cash flows. The company also does not have any debt maturities until 2028. During this time, the enterprise is focused not only on growing operations, but also on returning capital to shareholders. The business recently increased its quarterly dividend by 13%. And in 2024, management oversaw the repurchase of 4.7 million shares for about $150 million. That works out to roughly 3% of the shares that were outstanding at that time. What's more, the company still has about $240 million remaining under its share buyback program. In all likelihood, additional share purchases are in the cards. Considering how the stock is priced, I would much prefer management allocate this extra capital toward growth. But at least shares aren't overvalued.

Takeaway

From the data that I can see at this time, I do think that Zurn Elkay Water Solutions is an interesting business. But in all honesty, it's not for me. Despite attractive growth in recent years and a track record for reducing leverage while simultaneously returning capital to shareholders, the company isn't cheap enough to justify a bullish assessment. For now, the best thing that I can do is rate it a ‘hold’. I doubt that the upcoming earnings data will change that opinion. But it could.
